objective: Establish the seven men arriving at the lake house, the twenty-five-year tradition, and the group dynamics through reunion and settling in
opposition: Old grudges surfacing through seemingly casual catching-up; competitive edges sharpening
turn: From polite reunion energy to the first real jab — someone's divorce, someone's money, someone's weight
consequence_cost: Old wounds reopen under the guise of friendly ribbing; the group's fault lines are visible before the first beer is finished

objective: The fantasy football draft as a comedy set piece — the excuse for the weekend and the language through which these men express loyalty, grudge, and dominance
opposition: Everyone wants the same picks; Martin's controlling generosity clashes with Cliff's deal-making and Joel's simmering anger
turn: Martin's quiet authority over the group becomes visible when he overrides a disputed pick; Tommy gives Martin the Viagra as a joke
consequence_cost: Relationships strained by competition that is both trivial and deeply personal; Tommy's gift is a ticking clock the reader doesn't recognize

objective: Late night unraveling — the evening loosens into drinking, hot tub, old stories, and finally Nate discovering Martin dead in his bed
opposition: The evening's momentum toward sleep vs. Nate's impulse to find Martin for one last bet
turn: Nate finds Martin dead; the body is still warm; the comedy of the evening becomes retroactively horrifying
consequence_cost: The twenty-five-year ritual is shattered; the reader's relationship to everything that came before changes

objective: Paul finds Martin's body, attempts CPR, and confronts the reality that his best friend is dead
opposition: Death itself — Paul's desperate resuscitation against the irreversible
turn: Paul accepts Martin is dead; his grief is immediate, physical, and specific
consequence_cost: Loss of his closest friend; the beginning of Paul's night of irrational decisions

objective: Paul tampers with the scene and becomes aware that others are also moving through the house
opposition: Paul's rational self vs. his panic; the sounds of other people sneaking through the house
turn: Paul decides to reposition the body and wipe surfaces; by dawn the scene is unrecognizable
consequence_cost: Scene contaminated by Paul and at least four others; a natural death now looks like an elaborate homicide

objective: Tommy wakes to the news of Martin's death and realizes the Viagra he gave Martin may have contributed to cardiac arrest
opposition: Tommy's guilt colliding with his need to appear normal; his body betraying his panic
turn: Tommy decides to destroy the Viagra — his guilt overrides his judgment
consequence_cost: Evidence about to be destroyed; Tommy's pattern of impulsive panic-action established

objective: Tommy flushes the Viagra pills while Detective Mallick arrives at the house
opposition: Mallick's arrival audible through the bathroom door; Tommy is holding evidence as the detective enters
turn: Tommy is trapped in the bathroom — he drops a pill, chases it, flushes as the doorbell rings and Mallick's voice carries through the house
consequence_cost: Evidence destroyed; Tommy's proximity to Mallick at the moment of tampering creates future vulnerability

objective: Mallick's entrance — casserole, wrong name, bathroom request — establishes her persona for the guests and the reader
opposition: Social norms colliding with a death investigation; the guests' expectation of procedural seriousness vs. Mallick's apparent disorder
turn: Mallick's bumbling performance establishes; the guests relax, which is her intent
consequence_cost: The guests underestimate Mallick — they begin correcting her freely, each correction a gift of information

objective: Mallick's informal interrogation of Joel — the most obviously suspicious guest
opposition: Joel's legal training (silence) vs. his guilt about the argument (confession); Mallick's indirection vs. Joel's precision
turn: Mallick pins Joel as prime suspect through his own non-answers — his refusal to explain the argument becomes the loudest statement in the room
consequence_cost: Joel is now the most watched man in the house; his clean hands look calculated rather than innocent

objective: The group reacts to Mallick's presence and begins negotiating their collective presentation
opposition: Need to present a united front vs. individual secrets pulling each man toward self-preservation
turn: The group's attempt at solidarity fractures — small contradictions in their stories emerge as they try to coordinate
consequence_cost: Trust begins to erode; the facade of group loyalty shows cracks

objective: Mallick interviews Paul — her planted errors testing his knowledge of the scene
opposition: Paul's insurance-trained precision vs. Mallick's deliberately wrong details
turn: Paul corrects Mallick about the body's position — revealing he knows exactly how Martin was oriented, which means he saw the body before it was officially found
consequence_cost: Paul has inadvertently exposed his tampering through the instinct to be accurate

objective: Mallick interviews Cliff — his smoothness tested by her indirection
opposition: Cliff's sales-trained composure vs. Mallick's casual misdescription of the house layout
turn: Cliff corrects her about which room is Martin's study — revealing he was in a room he had no stated reason to enter
consequence_cost: Cliff's snooping is now flagged in Mallick's mental map; his financial motive is visible

objective: Mallick's private assessment of the interviews and the evidence so far
opposition: The evidence contradicting any clean theory vs. Mallick's need for a working framework
turn: Mallick concludes the scene was tampered with but cannot determine by whom or how many; her note: five men who slept through the night, and the house rearranged by morning
consequence_cost: More questions than answers; Mallick's investigation shifts from routine death to active puzzle

objective: Nate spirals over the deleted voicemail — trying to verify it is truly gone
opposition: Fear of discovery vs. the desire to act natural; technological uncertainty about whether deleted voicemails are recoverable
turn: Nate checks Martin's phone again and googles carrier retention policies, deepening his own paranoia
consequence_cost: Escalating anxiety; Nate's behavior becomes increasingly erratic and observable

objective: Nate's interrogation by Mallick — his compulsion to talk vs. his need to conceal the voicemail
opposition: Nate's performative, narrative-driven personality vs. the requirement to withhold specific information
turn: Nate accidentally references the voicemail he claims not to have left; Mallick thanks him for the correction
consequence_cost: Nate has effectively confessed to tampering; Mallick now knows about the deletion; Nate's podcaster instincts are his own worst enemy

objective: Doug's interior life — medication, divorce aftermath, the act of hiding his prescriptions from his oldest friends
opposition: Depression vs. the need to function; shame vs. the need for connection
turn: Doug's inner world revealed as rich, observant, and fragile beneath the group's quietest exterior
consequence_cost: The reader sees Doug fully for the first time; his vulnerability is established for the Ch 14 payoff

objective: Doug's interview with Mallick — the shortest and most suspicious
opposition: Doug's monosyllabic responses vs. Mallick's need for information; silence creating more suspicion than Nate's flood of words
turn: Mallick flags Doug's silence as unusual — the least talkative man becomes a person of interest
consequence_cost: Doug is now under scrutiny for being quiet, which is the cruelest irony for someone using silence as survival

objective: Cliff enters Martin's study and opens the safe, looking for documentation of Martin's forty-thousand-dollar debt
opposition: Risk of being caught vs. financial desperation; the safe's accessibility (combination left one tick off)
turn: Cliff finds the safe open and begins searching — financial documents, Martin's will, cash
consequence_cost: Time pressure and the vulnerability of being in a dead man's office during an investigation

objective: Cliff discovers the manila envelope labeled RT and reads Martin's account of the 2002 fire
opposition: Expectation (real estate documents) vs. reality (a twenty-two-year-old murder cover-up)
turn: Cliff reads about Tommy, the fire, and Ray Toomey's death; he photographs the document and replaces it
consequence_cost: Cliff now holds information worth far more than forty thousand dollars — and far more dangerous than he understands

objective: Group breakfast — the accumulated tension of the weekend pressing against the social ritual of eating together
opposition: The need for normalcy vs. every secret in the room; someone pushes Joel about the argument
turn: Joel is cornered — the group's patience with his silence has run out
consequence_cost: Joel must either explain or fracture the group's remaining cohesion

objective: Joel reveals that Martin was sleeping with his ex-wife Karen
opposition: Joel's pride and privacy vs. the group's demand for explanation; the dead man's betrayal now public
turn: The truth comes out; the group's loyalty to Martin splinters along multiple lines
consequence_cost: Joel's motive (rage at Martin) becomes sympathetically legible but the group's unity is destroyed

objective: The group debates the Martin-Karen revelation like a fantasy football ethics dispute
opposition: Loyalty to Martin vs. sympathy for Joel; each man's moral framework applied to a question of personal betrayal
turn: The argument reveals each man's values and blind spots — Tommy's coaching-mode certainty, Cliff's transactional framing, Paul's conflicted defense
consequence_cost: Allegiances shift; the group is no longer unified by grief but divided by judgment

objective: Mallick reviews evidence and realizes the tampering was done by multiple people independently
opposition: A coherent theory of the case vs. evidence that is too scattered and contradictory for one person
turn: The committee-that-never-met realization — five panic attacks, not one murder
consequence_cost: Mallick's theory fundamentally changes; she is now looking for five motives instead of one

objective: Mallick re-interviews with questions designed to test the multi-tamperer theory
opposition: The guests' composure vs. Mallick's new and more specific questioning angle
turn: Mallick tests her theory against someone's responses and confirms at least two independent tamperers
consequence_cost: The guests sense the shift in questioning; the investigation has a new shape

objective: Paul finds Martin's will in the study safe — Martin left Tommy the lake house
opposition: Paul's grief and his assumption that he was Martin's primary relationship vs. the will's evidence
turn: Martin left the house to Tommy, not Paul — the shock of being secondary in his best friend's priorities
consequence_cost: Paul's understanding of his twenty-five-year friendship shifts fundamentally

objective: Paul reviews twenty-five years of Martin-Tommy dynamics through memory and growing suspicion
opposition: Paul's loyal memories of Martin vs. the disturbing pattern of protectiveness, tolerance, and obligation toward Tommy
turn: Paul begins to suspect Martin was protecting Tommy from something specific and serious — the shape of it is legible across two decades of small choices
consequence_cost: Paul's grief is now mixed with suspicion; his relationship to Martin's memory is permanently altered

objective: Cliff confronts Tommy on the deck with the fire document as leverage
opposition: Cliff's calculated confidence vs. Tommy's desperate need to keep the secret; the thirty-year friendship warped by leverage
turn: Cliff names the fire, names Ray Toomey, shows he has the document — Tommy's world cracks
consequence_cost: Tommy is under direct external threat; his twenty-two-year-old secret is in a man's hands who sees it as a business opportunity

objective: Tommy's memory of the night of the fire — the cabin, the smoke, Ray Toomey, Martin's calm management of the aftermath
opposition: Past horror colliding with present danger; the weight of twenty-two years of guilt concentrated
turn: The fire is fully present — the reader sees what Tommy has been carrying and what Martin was managing
consequence_cost: The cost of Tommy's secret is embodied, not abstract; the reader understands the weight even as the comedy has concealed it

objective: Tommy agrees to Cliff's financial terms but something in his body shifts
opposition: Compliance vs. violence; the surface agreement masking an internal decision
turn: Tommy acquiesces verbally, but the physicality the reader has been trained to read as comedy is now legible as danger
consequence_cost: The murder motive is fully formed; Tommy's agreement is temporary and the reader may not yet know why it feels wrong

objective: The fantasy football waiver wire debate — should Martin's auto-drafted team stand, and who manages his roster
opposition: The absurdity of the debate vs. the real tensions underneath; each argument about fantasy football is a proxy for loyalty and obligation
turn: The debate becomes the group's only language for talking about what they owe each other and the dead
consequence_cost: The group's pretense of normalcy is audible and fragile; the comedy is genuine and poignant

objective: Last dinner, sunset on the lake — the final moment that feels like the old weekends
opposition: The desire to hold the moment vs. everything pulling it apart; Nate observes Tommy-Cliff distance, Doug's sobriety, Joel-Paul tension
turn: Nate catalogs the wrongness he can see but not name — the last stretch of comedy sitting on top of assembled danger
consequence_cost: The final comedy; the reader's enjoyment is tinged with awareness that the register is about to break

objective: Monday evening — the Martin investigation winding down, the group preparing to leave
opposition: Relief and premature closure vs. something unresolved that Doug can feel but not name
turn: The group begins packing and making departure plans; normalcy is almost restored
consequence_cost: The false sense of resolution makes what follows more devastating

objective: Doug goes to the dock for his evening ritual and discovers Cliff floating face-down in the water
opposition: Doug's need for quiet vs. the reality of what he finds; the dock from Ch 7 transformed from refuge to crime scene
turn: Doug finds Cliff dead — the wrong shoes, the head wound, the shallow water that couldn't drown a conscious person
consequence_cost: Everything changes; the comedy is over; the quietest man makes the sound that ends it

objective: Aftermath of the discovery — the group at the dock, the reality of a second death
opposition: Shock and denial vs. the physical evidence; the need to act vs. paralysis
turn: The group realizes this death is different — this was not natural causes
consequence_cost: The weekend is no longer survivable as a story they tell; one of them is a murderer

objective: Mallick returns to the lake house — no casserole, no wrong names, no folksy performance
opposition: The guests' expectation of the old Mallick vs. the detective who arrives; the gap between personas
turn: Mallick gets Martin's name right on the first try; the guests notice; the house is locked down as a crime scene
consequence_cost: False safety stripped away; the guests understand the stakes have changed categorically

objective: Mallick examines Cliff's body and the dock crime scene
opposition: Evidence vs. staging; the physical details that distinguish murder from accident
turn: The head wound inconsistent with a fall, water in lungs suggesting submersion, and the wrong shoes — this was staged by someone careless
consequence_cost: Forensic certainty: this is a homicide, committed by someone strong, desperate, and hasty

objective: Mallick conducts first round of murder interviews using the correction map from the Martin investigation
opposition: Guests' composure vs. Mallick's correction map applied to new questions about access, motive, and timeline
turn: Mallick's accumulation of corrections from the comic investigation becomes the operational tool — who had dock access, who was where, whose corrections revealed knowledge
consequence_cost: The comedy's data now serves the tragedy; the reader sees that Mallick has been building the case since the casserole

objective: Joel and Paul sit on the deck and compare what they each know about Martin, Tommy, and Cliff
opposition: What they know vs. what they can prove; Joel's legal caution vs. Paul's emotional certainty
turn: The picture begins to assemble — Martin's protection of Tommy, Cliff's discovery, the leverage, the motive
consequence_cost: Joel and Paul are building the same case Mallick is building, from the civilian side

objective: Joel and Paul go to the study, find the fire document, and read Martin's account of the 2002 cabin fire
opposition: Loyalty to Tommy and the group vs. the evidence of what Tommy did and what it cost
turn: Joel reads about the fire, Ray Toomey, and Martin's cover-up; the crease pattern shows the document was handled by multiple people; the full picture locks in
consequence_cost: Joel and Paul know Tommy killed Cliff to protect a twenty-two-year-old secret; they must decide what to do with the knowledge

objective: Paul confronts Tommy alone on the deck
opposition: Paul's grief and love for Martin vs. Tommy's desperation and exhaustion; the twenty-five-year friendship collapsing under the weight of truth
turn: Paul tells Tommy he knows — about the fire, about Cliff, about all of it; Tommy's response cycles through denial, anger, and something that looks like relief
consequence_cost: Twenty-five years of friendship end in a conversation on the deck where they've had a thousand conversations before

objective: Tommy's response — the weight of twenty-two years surfacing
opposition: Tommy's self-preservation instincts vs. the exhaustion of carrying Ray Toomey for two decades; denial vs. the relief of being seen
turn: Paul tells Tommy that Martin left him the house; Tommy breaks — not dramatically, but as a man realizing the chain was also the thing holding him together
consequence_cost: Tommy's collapse is quiet and complete; the Viagra guilt, the fire guilt, Martin's death, Cliff's death — all of it arrives at once

objective: Paul's ultimatum — Tommy has until morning to go to Mallick
opposition: Paul's decision not to protect Tommy the way Martin did vs. Tommy's last hope for escape
turn: Paul gives Tommy the night; Tommy sits alone on the deck of the house he just learned he inherited
consequence_cost: The last choice is Tommy's; Paul has broken the cycle of protection that Martin maintained for twenty-two years

objective: Tommy comes to Mallick in the kitchen at dawn and tells her about Cliff's murder
opposition: Tommy's silence vs. his exhaustion; the legal instinct not to confess vs. the need to stop carrying it
turn: Tommy tells Mallick about the dock, the push, the water; he does not confess to the fire; Mallick reads him his rights
consequence_cost: Tommy arrested; the investigation resolves; the real crime was the one the comedy was hiding

objective: Aftermath — Tommy taken away, the surviving men in the house, Mallick alone in the kitchen
opposition: The ending vs. what endures; the group's future vs. the group's destruction
turn: The casserole is still in the fridge; the draft board still on the wall; Martin's picks circled in red; the league is over
consequence_cost: The final image is quiet — a kitchen, an untouched casserole, a draft board for a league that will never play again
